text: AI Playbook and Toolkit for Public Health Departments
textPreview: AI Playbook and Toolkit for Public Health Departments

alt: AI Playbook for Public Health logo

text:
Welcome participants to the Responsible Prompting and Review lesson.
Explain that this deck is designed for staff training, leadership orientation, governance preparation, or cross-program discussion.
Frame the topic in plain language: Responsible prompting helps staff use generative AI safely by protecting data, asking precise questions, requiring sources when needed, and reviewing outputs before use.
Invite participants to think about where this topic already appears in their department's workflows, vendor tools, data systems, communications, or planning conversations.
Responsible prompting helps staff use generative AI safely by protecting data, asking precise questions, requiring sources when needed, and reviewing outputs before use.

text: Responsible prompting and review reduce the chance that staff use AI in ways that expose data, spread misinformation, create inaccessible communication, or bypass expert review.
textPreview: Responsible prompting and review reduce the chance that staff use AI in ways that expose data, spread misinformation, create inaccessible...

text: A polished AI response can still be inaccurate, biased, outdated, unsupported, or inappropriate for public health use.
textPreview: A polished AI response can still be inaccurate, biased, outdated, unsupported, or inappropriate for public health use.

text:
Use this slide in the Responsible Prompting and Review training module.
Use this slide to explain the practical public health reason for learning this topic before AI tools are approved or deployed.
Connect the discussion back to the playbook: this topic supports Plays 1, 3, 5, 6, 10, 11 and Tools 2, 14, 23, 28, 29, 40.
Emphasize the core guardrail: A polished AI response can still be inaccurate, biased, outdated, unsupported, or inappropriate for public health use.
Ask participants how this topic could affect their own program area, staff roles, community relationships, data, or public communications.
By the end of this lesson, staff should be able to:
textPreview: By the end of this lesson, staff should be able to:

text:
• Explain Responsible Prompting and Review in plain language.
• Identify appropriate public health uses and limits.
• Describe human review, governance, equity, privacy, and monitoring expectations.
• Connect the topic to the related plays and tools.

text:
Use this slide in the Responsible Prompting and Review training module.
Walk through the objectives and connect each one to a practical implementation decision.
Connect the discussion back to the playbook: this topic supports Plays 1, 3, 5, 6, 10, 11 and Tools 2, 14, 23, 28, 29, 40.
Explain that participants do not need technical expertise to use this material. The goal is enough shared understanding to participate in governance, readiness assessment, use case review, procurement, deployment, or monitoring.

Staff need practical guidance for everyday generative AI use. Prompting is not magic language; it is a structured way to define task, audience, source material, constraints, review criteria, and prohibited data. The review step is more important than the prompt.

text:
Use this slide in the Responsible Prompting and Review training module.
Use this slide to make the topic concrete through public health workflow examples.
Connect the discussion back to the playbook: this topic supports Plays 1, 3, 5, 6, 10, 11 and Tools 2, 14, 23, 28, 29, 40.
For each example, ask: What problem is being solved? What data would be used? Who reviews the AI output? What decision remains human? What could go wrong?
Which AI-assisted drafting tasks should be allowed for staff? Which outputs require source verification, supervisor review, or communications approval? How should staff document AI-assisted work?

text: Rewrite three unsafe prompts into responsible prompts, then review sample AI outputs for data exposure, hallucinations, tone, equity concerns, missing sources, and required approvals.
